Cheat Sheet

Isabel Leiva

April 2026

Coding Cheat Sheet

Functions, arguments, techniques, and their uses. Organized first by most common usage and secondarily by package they are contained in.

Dataframe Building and Editing

Base R 

#help()
help("function") #run help in console to get detailed information in bottom right pane

#c()
mary_sentence <- c("MARY HAD A LITTLE LAMB") #combines values into a single vector

#tolower
mary_sentence <- tolower(mary_sentence) #makes all lower case, but also turns back into a vector

#data.frame()
mary_df <- data.frame(mary_sentence) #creates a dataframe. columns may be of differing types (numeric, logical, factor, and character).

#gsub()
gsub("mary", "Mary",mary_df$mary_sentence) #allows you to substitute a value with something else
## [1] "Mary had a little lamb"
#data()
data(stop_words) #used to load datasets that come pre-installed with R packages or datasets that have been explicitly installed into your R environment. "stop_words" come from the tidytext dataset

#indexing
mary_df[1,] # allows you to specify columns and rows by number [row,column]. Colons indicates "through" (e.g 1 through 3)
## [1] "mary had a little lamb"
#function()
myfunc<- function(x){ #allows you to create a function that can apply multiple conditions, functions, and actions to a list, vector,or data frame. 
  y <- x + 3
  print(y)
} 
myfunc(3) #running new function
## [1] 6
#sapply()
numbers <- data.frame( x=c(1,2,3,4,5,6),
                       y=c(3,2,4,2,34,5)) #creating df for example
sapply(numbers, mean, na.rm = TRUE) #applies a single function to a list, vector, or data frame.
##        x        y 
## 3.500000 8.333333
#str()
str(numbers) # describes the structure of an object in r. For a dataframe it gives the number of observations, variables, column names, data type (e.g., int, num, chr), and sample values
## 'data.frame':    6 obs. of  2 variables:
##  $ x: num  1 2 3 4 5 6
##  $ y: num  3 2 4 2 34 5

Natural Language Processing

tidytext 

#unnest_tokens()
mary_unnested <- mary_df %>% unnest_tokens(word, mary_sentence) #Splits a column into tokens, flattening the table into one-token-per-row

dplyr 

#mutate()
mary_df %>% mutate(sentence = mary_sentence) #create, modify, and delete columns
mary_sentence sentence
mary had a little lamb mary had a little lamb 
#anti_join
mary_unnested %>% anti_join(stop_words) #compares one dataframe to another and removes words that match
x
mary
lamb 
#count
mary_unnested %>% count(word, sort = TRUE) #counts unique words in df
word n
a 1
had 1
lamb 1
little 1
mary 1

textstem 

#lemmatize_strings
textstem::lemmatize_strings(mary_sentence) #lemmatizes text
## [1] "mary have a little lamb"

tidyverse 

#tibble()
mary_df <- tibble(mary_sentence) #creates a "smart" dataframe that works well with knitting

quanteda 

# corpus()
mary_corpus <- mary_df %>% 
  mutate(sentence = mary_sentence) %>% #create, modify, and delete columns
  quanteda::corpus(text_field = "sentence") #using the quanteda::corpus() function to convert df into a corpus. The argument text_field() tells quanteda which column of the df to summarize as a corpus.

# tokens()
mary_token <- quanteda::tokens(mary_corpus, remove_punct = TRUE) #Creates a tokenized version of the corpus or df

# textstat_lexdiv()
textstat_lexdiv(mary_token, measure = c("TTR"), log.base = 10) #lexical diversity, can take a dfm, corpus or token
document TTR
text1 1 
#dfm()
mary_dfm <- dfm(mary_token) # Create a document-feature matrix

# selecting the top features from the dfm
topfeatures(mary_dfm, 5) #top 5 most used words 
##   mary    had      a little   lamb 
##      1      1      1      1      1

SemanticDistance

Andy R package: Link to our R package here 

#creating dataframe for examples
isabag <- data.frame(mytext = c("scaffold", "building", "church", "concrete", "spackle", "beam", "construct", "hammer",
           "ship", "alien", "planet", "space", "star", "comet", "crater", "extraterrestrial"))

#clean_monologue_or_list()
MyCleanList <- clean_monologue_or_list(dat=isabag, wordcol='mytext') #cleans single column of words

#wordlist_to_network()
mydendro <- wordlist_to_network(MyCleanList, output='dendrogram', dist_type='SD15') #creates visual semantic distance clustering network

#dist_ngram2word()
Ngram2Word_Dists1 <- dist_ngram2word(dat=MyCleanList, ngram=1) #cosine distance word-to-word, comparing each word to the word directly before it
Ngram2Word_Dists1
id_row_orig text_initialsplit word_clean id_row_postsplit CosDist_1gram_glo CosDist_1gram_sd15
1 scaffold scaffold 1 NA NA
2 building build 2 0.79 0.35
3 church church 3 0.61 0.80
4 concrete concrete 4 0.73 1.02
5 spackle spackle 5 0.90 0.18
6 beam beam 6 0.93 0.65
7 construct construct 7 0.87 0.84
8 hammer hammer 8 0.90 0.45
9 ship ship 9 0.81 0.65
10 alien alien 10 0.55 0.98
11 planet planet 11 0.51 0.67
12 space space 12 0.44 0.28
13 star star 13 0.55 0.10
14 comet comet 14 0.70 1.28
15 crater crater 15 0.67 0.21
16 extraterrestrial extraterrestrial 16 0.86 1.01 
#creating dataframe for examples
isabag_paired <- data.frame(word1 = c("scaffold", "building", "church", "concrete", "spackle", "beam", "construct", "hammer"),
                     word2 = c("ship", "alien", "planet", "space", "star", "comet", "crater", "extraterrestrial"))

#clean_paired_cols()
WordPairs_Clean <- clean_paired_cols(dat=isabag_paired, wordcol1='word1', wordcol2='word2') #cleans multiple columns of words
#dist_paired_cols()

Columns_Dists <- dist_paired_cols(WordPairs_Clean) #cosine distance word-to-word across two columns. Only argument is dataframe. Columns must be names "word1_clean" and "word2_clean"
Columns_Dists
id_row_orig word1_clean word2_clean word1 word2 CosDist_SD15 CosDist_GLO
1 scaffold ship scaffold ship 0.55 0.96
2 build alien building alien 1.10 0.75
3 church planet church planet 1.22 0.79
4 concrete space concrete space 0.36 0.59
5 spackle star spackle star 0.77 1.10
6 beam comet beam comet 0.75 0.76
7 construct crater construct crater 1.57 0.87
8 hammer extraterrestrial hammer extraterrestrial 1.31 1.05

Graphing

ggplot

Note: the data must be in long format 

# Create data
data <- data.frame( #creating a dataframe
  Story=c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A", "B", "C", "D","A", "B", "C", "D"),  #indicating column and content
  Score=c(8,5,3,0,8,5,3,0,8,5,3,0) #indicating column and content
  )
ggplot(data, aes(x = Story, y = Score, fill = Story)) + #specifying variables for each axis, fill argument indicates aesthetic differences
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", stat = "summary") + #specifying statistical analysis for bar graph (defaults to mean/sd)
  geom_errorbar(stat="summary",position=position_dodge(.55), width=0.1, size=.8) + #creating an error bar
  geom_hline(yintercept=4, linetype = "dashed") + #creating a line to indicate the "average"  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c('A' = '#D81B60', 'B' = '#1E88E5', 'C' = '#FFC107', 'D' = '#004D40')) + #specifying what color represents each variable specified in "fill"
  scale_x_discrete(labels=c("Charlotte's Web", "Babysitters Club", "Alice in Wonderland", 'Dune')) + #creating clearer names for each variable on the x-axis
  theme(legend.position = "none") + #removing legend since there is no tertiary variable
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,9), breaks=c(0, 2, 4, 7, 9)) + #specifying y-axis limits
  labs (title = "Book Appropriateness Ratings for Children",
        x = "Book",
        y = "Appropriateness") #creating labels for each part of the graph
